abstract: This paper proposes a method for measuring 'narrative similarity' across documents — the extent to which texts make the same claims about the same events — using large language models to distill texts into core claims and subjects before performing pairwise comparisons. The authors combine concept-guided chain-of-thought summarization, SBERT-based candidate pair generation, and zero-shot or fine-tuned GPT-4o annotation to make the approach tractable at scale. They validate the method against exact text reuse, topic modeling, standalone SBERT, and a relational (Relatio) approach using hand-coded gold standard data, then apply it to track the diffusion of Russian claims about Ukrainian U.S.-funded 'biolabs' across Russian state, Ukrainian, mainstream U.S., and low-quality U.S. news sources in early 2022.
key_claims: Narrative similarity — whether texts make the same claims about the same events — is a distinct estimand from lexical overlap, topical similarity, or semantic similarity, and existing methods conflate these.

An LLM-based pairwise pipeline that distills documents into claims and subjects can measure narrative similarity more faithfully than text reuse, topic modeling, semantic role labeling/Relatio, or standalone SBERT.
Exact text reuse methods have high precision but severely limited recall (under 7%), making them inappropriate for studying narrative diffusion outside direct syndication.
Fine-tuning the LLM annotator on purposively sampled boundary cases substantially improves precision (from 37% to ~79%) with only modest recall loss, yielding the best F1.

Low-quality U.S. news websites were substantially more likely than mainstream U.S. outlets to publish stories sharing narratives with Russian state media on the Ukrainian 'biolabs' story (14.1% vs 5.4%).

Exact text reuse estimators would have entirely missed the phenomenon of mainstream U.S. outlets sharing narratives with Russian state media, demonstrating that estimator choice has substantive downstream consequences.
methods: Assembled a multilingual corpus of 692,560 news articles from 45 sources (4 Russian state, 18 mainstream U.S., 11 low-quality U.S., 12 Ukrainian) covering Jan 1–Apr 30, 2022.
Filtered to 3,491 articles related to Ukrainian bioweapons claims using keyword filtering.

Used GPT-4o with concept-guided chain-of-thought prompts to (a) summarize each article in 7–10 English sentences, (b) enumerate subjects (people, places, objects, events), and (c) enumerate descriptive, normative, causal, and conceptual claims.
Generated candidate pairs using a two-stage SBERT pipeline: bi-encoder cosine similarity (cutoff 0.7) followed by cross-encoder scoring (cutoff 0.5), reducing 6.09M possible pairs to ~64,677.

Used GPT-4o zero-shot prompts to label whether candidate pairs shared the same subjects and the same claims, then fine-tuned a version using purposively sampled boundary cases (622 pairs, 161 positive/451 negative).

Validated via human annotation: built a recall set of 1,631 ranked-and-coded pairs (121 positive) plus a 47-pair holdout, and used majority-vote annotation of randomly sampled positive predictions to estimate precision.

Compared the method against four alternative estimators: 5-gram cosine similarity text reuse, structural topic modeling with binarized topic vectors, standalone SBERT cross-encoder, and Relatio-based semantic role labeling features.
findings: Fine-tuned SBERT-LLM achieved the best overall performance (F1=60.4, precision=78.8, recall=48.9 on the holdout).
Zero-shot SBERT-LLM achieved the highest recall (66.0%) but lower precision (37.0%), F1=47.4.
Exact 5-gram text reuse at 0.2 cosine similarity achieved only 6.4% recall despite 52.7% precision (F1=11.4).
Topic modeling clustering achieved recall of 27.7% and precision of 10.0% (F1=14.7).
Relatio-based estimator achieved recall of 34.0% but precision of only 10.5% (F1=16.0).
Standalone SBERT cross-encoder reached F1=35.6 — better than other alternatives but well below the full pipeline.
In the bioweapons application, 14.1% of low-quality U.S. articles shared narratives with Russian state media versus 5.4% of mainstream articles.

The ngram estimator detected zero mainstream U.S. articles sharing narratives with Russian state media, whereas SBERT-LLM detected ~10% on average — revealing a phenomenon invisible to lexical methods.
Coverage spiked around March 11 (Russia's UN Security Council request) and March 24 (Hunter Biden funding allegations).
contributions: Introduces 'narrative similarity' as a precisely defined estimand distinct from lexical, semantic, or topical similarity, grounded in the sociology of narrative.
Provides a scalable three-stage pipeline (LLM distillation → SBERT candidate filtering → LLM pairwise annotation) for measuring narrative similarity across large multilingual corpora.

Develops an out-of-sample validation strategy — including a ranked human-in-the-loop recall set with a stopping rule — that yields supervised-learning metrics (precision, recall, F1) for an effectively unsupervised task.
Demonstrates substantial gains from purposive-sample fine-tuning on boundary cases.
Provides empirical evidence on the laundering of Russian state media narratives by low-quality versus mainstream U.S. news outlets during the 2022 Ukraine invasion.
Offers a general framework applicable to studying propaganda, misinformation, policy diffusion, and cultural diffusion beyond the specific case.
framing: The paper positions itself at the intersection of computational sociology, political communication, and international relations research on strategic narratives and narrative diffusion, while contributing to the methodological literature on text-as-data and LLM-based measurement. It engages explicitly with debates about estimand-estimator alignment (Lundberg et al. 2021) and with the validation challenges of unsupervised text methods (Grimmer and King 2011), proposing LLMs as a tool that finally makes faithful measurement of narrative-level similarity feasible.
